title: Basic String Operations
description: Manipulate character strings with paste, nchar, substr, etc.

slides.title: Common String Functions 🔤
slides.content:
R has many functions to work with strings:

* `nchar(x)` – number of characters in a string
* `toupper(x)` / `tolower(x)` – change case
* `paste(...)` – concatenate strings with a separator
* `paste0(...)` – concatenate without separator
* `substr(x, start, stop)` – extract substring
* `strsplit(x, split)` – split string into list
* `grep()`, `sub()`, `gsub()` – pattern matching and replacement (regex)

Let's explore the most common ones.

slides.title: paste and paste0
slides.content:
`paste()` combines strings with a default separator (space). `paste0()` uses no separator.

```r
paste("Hello", "World")      # "Hello World"
paste0("Hello", "World")     # "HelloWorld"
paste("a", "b", sep = "-")   # "a-b"
```

You can also collapse a vector:

```r
paste(c("x", "y"), collapse = "")  # "xy"
```

slides.title: paste Examples
slides.codeSnippet:
first <- "John"
last <- "Doe"
full <- paste(first, last)
print(full)

ids <- paste("ID", 1:3, sep = "_")
print(ids)

combined <- paste(ids, collapse = ", ")
print(combined)

quizData.question: What is the output of `paste(1:3, collapse = "+")`?
quizData.explanation: collapse combines the vector elements with the specified separator, so "1+2+3".

slides.title: Substrings with substr
slides.content:
`substr(x, start, stop)` extracts a portion of a string:

```r
text <- "programming"
substr(text, 1, 4)   # "prog"
substr(text, 5, 7)   # "ram"
```

You can also assign to a substring:

```r
substr(text, 1, 4) <- "PROG"
```

slides.title: Pro Tip 🚀
slides.content: For more advanced string manipulation, check out the `stringr` package (part of tidyverse). It provides consistent functions like `str_length()`, `str_c()`, `str_sub()`, etc.

slides.title: String Challenge
slides.content: Create a vector of first names: "Alice", "Bob", "Charlie". Create a vector of last names: "Smith", "Jones", "Brown". Paste them together with a space to get full names. Then convert all to uppercase.
slides.codeSnippet:
first <- c("Alice", "Bob", "Charlie")
last <- c("Smith", "Jones", "Brown")
full_names <- paste(first, last)
print(full_names)
upper_names <- toupper(full_names)
print(upper_names)
